1: Chapter 11 opens a new unit: the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH. The covenant-lawsuit form that follows is one of the most developed in the prophets. Jeremiah is commissioned to proclaim the covenant — specifically, the Deuteronomic covenant that Josiah had reinstituted following the discovery of the law-scroll in 621 BC (2 Kgs 22–23). The chapter thus presupposes a post-Josiah setting, likely early in Jehoiakim's reign when the reforms had already begun to unravel.
2: Hear the words of this covenant — the word berit (covenant) will appear seven times in vv1–11, structuring the passage as a covenant-proclamation. Jeremiah is not composing new theology; he is re-announcing the Mosaic covenant in a context where it has been systematically violated. The repetition of berit functions both as reminder and as indictment — every mention is an implicit accusation.
3: And say to them, Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Cursed be the man who does not hear the words of this covenant. The curse formula inverts the blessing of Deuteronomy 28: where Deuteronomy opened with barak (blessed), Jeremiah's covenant proclamation opens with arur (cursed). The curse is not new law but the already-announced consequence of covenant violation. Jeremiah is announcing that the cursed state has arrived.
4: …which I commanded your fathers when I brought them out of the land of Egypt, from the iron furnace — Egypt as iron furnace (kur habarzel) is a Deuteronomic image (Deuteronomy 4:20; 1 Kings 8:51) depicting the severity of Israelite slavery. The covenant was forged in the context of liberation: YHWH freed a people from the worst form of human bondage and then entered covenant with them. Listen to my voice and do all that I command you. So shall you be my people, and I will be your God — the covenant formula in its simplest bilateral form.
5: That I may confirm the oath that I swore to your fathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey — the patriarchal promise (Genesis 12:1–3, Jeremiah 15:18–21, Jeremiah 22:15–18) stands behind the Sinai covenant. The Exodus-covenant is the mechanism by which the patriarchal land-promise is fulfilled. Jeremiah is tracing the full theological line from Abraham to Sinai to Canaan, demonstrating that covenant violation now threatens the entire promise-chain. As at this day: the land had been given, was being occupied — covenant violation now threatens that possession.
6: Then YHWH said to me, Proclaim all these words in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem — Jeremiah is sent as a circuit-preacher of the covenant, traveling to every city and the capital. This is consistent with his role throughout the book as covenant-prosecutor. The instruction to proclaim in the streets of Jerusalem suggests the message is not restricted to Temple precincts; it is public, confrontational, unavoidable.
7: For I solemnly warned your fathers when I brought them up out of the land of Egypt, warning them persistently, even to this day — the repeated sending of prophets is a characteristic Deuteronomistic theme (cf. 2 Kings 17:13; Jeremiah 7:25; Jeremiah 25:4; Jeremiah 26:5). YHWH's patience is not passivity — he has been warning, sending messengers, calling for return. The failure is entirely on the human side. Saying, Obey my voice: the covenant demand is simple; the failure to meet it is a choice, not an accident.
8: Yet they did not obey or incline their ear, but everyone walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart. The sherirut lev hara' (stubbornness of the evil heart) is a recurring Jeremianic diagnosis (Jeremiah 7:24; Jeremiah 13:10; Jeremiah 16:12; Jeremiah 18:12; Jeremiah 23:17). The stubbornness is not merely behavioral but anthropological — the heart itself has been corrupted toward self-will. Therefore I brought upon them all the words of this covenant, which I commanded them to do, but they did not: the curses of Deuteronomy 28 are now being implemented.
9: Again YHWH said to me: A conspiracy exists among the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. The term qesher (conspiracy, binding together) is normally used of political plots (2 Kings 12:20; Jeremiah 14:19). Here it describes the collective apostasy of Judah and Jerusalem as a deliberate organized rebellion — not accidental drift but coordinated rejection of the covenant. The language heightens the culpability: this is not ignorance but intentional betrayal.
10: They have turned back to the iniquities of their forefathers, who refused to hear my words; they have gone after other gods to serve them. The generational structure of apostasy: the present generation has not invented a new sin but returned to the ancestral pattern. The house of Israel and the house of Judah have broken my covenant that I made with their fathers: both kingdoms are indicted. This matters in Jeremiah's context because the northern kingdom had already fallen to Assyria in 722 BC — Judah has not learned from that catastrophic judgment.
11: Therefore, thus says YHWH: Behold, I am bringing disaster upon them that they cannot escape — ra'ah (disaster, evil) is a covenant-judgment term. The declaration that they cannot escape answers the false security embedded in the Zion-theology (the assumption that YHWH will always protect Jerusalem). Though they cry to me, I will not listen to them: covenant prayer requires covenant fidelity. YHWH's non-response to prayer is not capriciousness but covenant logic — they have broken the relationship that would make prayer meaningful.
12: Then the cities of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem will go and cry to the gods to whom they make offerings — the bitter irony: when YHWH stops listening, they will turn to the idols they have preferred. But they cannot save them in the time of their trouble: the crisis will expose the idols' impotence. The people will discover that the alternative gods they pursued cannot perform the one function most needed — deliverance. This is the idol-critique of chapter 10 now applied to historical judgment.
13: For your gods have become as many as your cities, O Judah, and as many as the streets of Jerusalem are the altars you have set up to shame, altars to make offerings to Baal. The proliferation of altars mirrors the proliferation of apostasy: every city has its own local shrine, every street its own altar. The phrase altars to shame (mizbachot laboshet) is a deliberate substitution — boshet (shame) replaces the deity's name, a polemical editorial convention used elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ish-bosheth = Ish-Baal, Mephibosheth = Merib-Baal). Baal worship is literally named as shame.
14: As for you, do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer on their behalf, for I will not listen when they call to me in the time of their trouble. The prohibition of intercession (cf. Jeremiah 7:16; Jeremiah 14:11; Jeremiah 15:1) is one of the most theologically striking elements in Jeremiah. YHWH is withdrawing the intercessory access that Moses, Samuel, and the prophets had exercised on Israel's behalf (cf. Jeremiah 15:1: even if Moses and Samuel stood before me). The prohibition signals that the covenant has been violated beyond the point where intercession can operate — not because prayer is powerless, but because the relational conditions for prayer have been destroyed.
15: What right has my beloved in my house, when she has done many vile deeds? — the marriage imagery of the covenant surfaces: Israel is YHWH's beloved (yedidah), but she has defiled the house (the Temple) with her vile deeds. Can the vows and sacrificial flesh avert your doom?: the question is sarcastic — the cultic system cannot function as a substitute for covenant fidelity. The Temple sacrifices Judah continues to offer cannot ward off the judgment they have earned by their apostasy.
16: YHWH once called you 'a green olive tree, beautiful with good fruit' — the olive tree is one of the most valuable and long-lived of Palestinian cultivars, associated with prosperity, peace, and permanence (Psalms 52:8). The past-tense once called is devastating: the beautiful description is historical, not current. With the roar of a great tempest he will set fire to it, and its branches will be consumed: the same tree YHWH called beautiful will be incinerated. The covenant people who were cultivated will be destroyed by the very God who tended them.
17: YHWH of hosts, who planted you, has decreed disaster against you, because of the evil that the house of Israel and the house of Judah have done, provoking me to anger by making offerings to Baal. YHWH as the planter and YHWH as the destroyer of the same tree frames the entire covenant relationship: he is not destroying something foreign to himself but his own planting. The logic of covenant judgment requires that the one who planted must also carry out the consequences of the covenant curse. Baal-worship is explicitly named as the trigger.
18: YHWH made it known to me and I knew; then you showed me their deeds — the narrative shifts into biographical material: Jeremiah describes his discovery that the people of Anathoth (his hometown) were plotting against his life. The transition from covenant-proclamation to personal persecution is not incidental — Jeremiah's bodily endangerment enacts what he prophetically announces: the rejection of the covenant word will cost the messenger his life, just as it will cost the nation its existence.
19: But I was like a gentle lamb led to the slaughter. I did not know it was against me they devised schemes, saying, 'Let us destroy the tree with its fruit; let us cut him off from the land of the living'. The gentle lamb led to slaughter becomes one of the OT's richest images, explicitly echoed in Isaiah 53:7 (he was led like a lamb to the slaughter) and applied to Jesus in John 1:29 and Acts 8:32. Jeremiah's innocent suffering at the hands of those who reject YHWH's word becomes a template for the Suffering Servant. The conspirators' phrase — cut him off from the land of the living — is the language of judicial murder.
20: But, O YHWH of hosts, who judges righteously, who tests the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause. Jeremiah's response to the conspiracy is not retaliation but covenant appeal. He commits his case (riv, his legal dispute) to YHWH as judge. The prayer that YHWH will vindicate him is not personal vengeance but covenant justice — the covenant God must act against those who violate his word and persecute his messenger. Tests the heart and mind (lev vekilyot, heart and kidneys) — YHWH sees motivations inaccessible to human courts.
21: Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the men of Anathoth who seek your life, and say, 'Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH, or you will die by our hand' — the threat is explicit and direct: cease prophesying or be killed. Anathoth was a Levitical city in Benjamin; the priestly community there likely resented Jeremiah's attacks on the Temple establishment and saw his prophecies as dangerous. Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH: the command to silence is itself a covenant violation — suppressing YHWH's word is the deeper crime underlying the murder plot.
22: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I will punish them. The young men shall die by the sword, their sons and daughters shall die by famine. YHWH's response to the conspiracy against his prophet is covenant judgment applied specifically to the conspirators: the sword and famine — two of the standard covenant-curse triad (sword, famine, plague; cf. Jeremiah 14:12; Jeremiah 21:9; Jeremiah 24:10). The specificity of the punishment — young men by sword, sons and daughters by famine — enacts the totality of household destruction.
23: And none of them shall be left. For I will bring disaster upon the men of Anathoth, the year of their punishment. The phrase shanah pequdatam (year of their punishment/visitation) is a Jeremianic technical term for the time of divine reckoning (cf. Jeremiah 8:12; Jeremiah 23:12; Jeremiah 48:44). The word paqad (visit, attend to, reckon with) can be positive (YHWH attending to his people in mercy) or negative (YHWH reckoning with their crimes). Here it is the latter: a fixed appointment with judgment from which there is no escape. The chapter ends with the persecutors fully condemned.
